compressed_prompt: night-sector commandos story, no caps, no 'coop'
response:
the air in the drop-ship tasted of ozone and cold metal. we were past the point of talking. the only sounds were the thrum of the engines and the click-check of gear. the mission was simple: penetrate, extract, vanish. the city below was a cancer of light, but our target was in the hollow dark at its heart—a research black-site designated “lighthouse.”

the light died as we fell. the chute opened with a soft crack, a sound swallowed by the void. we touched down on a derelict monorail track, our boots making no more sound than dust settling. the city’s glow was a distant halo, but here, in the night-sector, the dark was absolute, textured only by the sigh of the wind through skeletal structures.

mira was on point. her thermal overlay painted the world in ghosts of blue and green. she held up a fist. a patrol. two figures, their heat signatures blooming in the cold. we melted into the shadows, becoming part of the rust and concrete. they passed, chatting about a football match, oblivious to the five statues of death holding their breath three meters away.

we moved like oil through water. down service elevators with dead power, through sub-basements ankle-deep in stagnant chill. the facility was older than the gleaming city above, a forgotten root. the entry point was a maintenance conduit, its seal brittle with age. river, our demo man, applied a bead of acidic polymer. it hissed, eating through the metal without a flash of light or spark. he peeled back a perfect, silent circle.

inside, the air was sterile and dry. the walls were a dull grey, lit by the occasional red standby light. this was the place. the intelligence was vague—a scientist, dr. aris, had built something here. a “paradigm shifter.” command wanted it, and him. they didn’t specify which was priority.

we found the central lab. it was a cathedral of dormant machinery, all converging on a central plinth. on it rested not a weapon, but a smooth, obsidian sphere, about the size of a human head. it drank the light.

dr. aris was there, a slight man in a rumpled lab coat, staring at the sphere. he didn’t jump when we ghosted in. he just said, “you’re too late. or perhaps, right on time.”

“you’re coming with us,” i said, my voice a dry rustle in the filtered air. “the artifact as well.”

he turned. his eyes were tired. “it’s not an artifact. it’s a key. it turns everything off.”

a siren began to wail, not in our facility, but somewhere far above, muffled by layers of earth and steel. the red lights switched to a frantic blue strobe.

“they know you’re here,” aris said. “they’ll flood the sector with light. you’ll have no shadows left.”

mira’s voice came tight over the comms. “heat signatures. dozens. converging. not patrols. heavy armor.”

the plan was ash. extraction was compromised. river looked at me, his face grim in the strobing blue. “new objective?”

aris spoke again, his hand hovering near the sphere. “i built it to end wars. silence every engine, kill every circuit. a forced peace. but they want a switchblade, not a olive branch.”

the thunder of boots echoed down the corridor. they were coming fast, no attempt at stealth.

i looked at the sphere. i looked at the doctor. command’s priorities were unclear. in the night-sector, you make your own calls.

“river, rig the sphere. thermite paste. we don’t leave it for them.”

river nodded, already moving. aris sagged, a strange relief on his face.

“mira, find us a new path. not up. deeper.”

she was already scanning, pointing to a grating on the floor. “service tunnel. leads to the old flood channels.”

the first concussion grenade shook the door. river slapped the thermite onto the sphere, a grey lump that would burn hotter than hell. “sixty seconds,” he said.

we dropped through the grating as the lab door blew in. the last thing i saw was the sphere beginning to glow a furious, blinding white.

the flood channel was a river of icy black water. we waded, the world above shaking with the fury of our pursuers and the silent, devouring fire we had left behind. then, the shaking stopped. the sirens died. the few distant lights visible through grates above us winked out. the entire sector, perhaps the entire city-grid, plunged into a deeper, truer dark.

in that new silence, broken only by the splash of our movement, aris whispered, “the key is turned.”

we emerged at the edge of a concrete river, under a dead sky. the city of light was a tomb of shadows. our extraction was gone, its systems fried. we were stranded in a world we had just un-made.

mira checked her dead scope. river tested his dead comms. i looked at the team, then at the endless dark ahead.

“we walk,” i said. “the night is ours now. all of it.”

and we vanished into it, five ghosts in a dead world, the only commandos left in a silent war that had just lost its voice.
